# Transcrição: 15- Telling Your Story in Scenes

- **Fonte:** 15- Telling Your Story in Scenes
- **Duração:** 08:27
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Okay, I want to talk about harnessing the power of location a little bit more. We're going to talk about the middle of your story now. Now that we've locked in a beginning and an end, we've created a great frame. We've excited our audience with a great beginning and fulfilled the promise of that excitement with an outstanding ending. Let's talk about what we're going to do in the middle. The first thing we have to think about is how we're going to structure our story and I want you to structure it in scenes. Essentially what I want you to do is this. At every moment in your story, your audience should be able to identify your physical location. The same way that we started our story with location and then action, we're going to start each separate scene of our story with the same. So when we think about the spoon of power story, I started in a playground chasing a boy after a spoon and then I moved into a classroom where I was teaching a math lesson and then I shifted in the classroom a little bit to teach a reading lesson and then I shifted in the classroom again to teach a writing lesson. Then Jamie left the classroom stopping by the library as he left out of the hall and then the next day we were back in the classroom again. He put the spoon around Mackenzie's neck one day. He waited by the door one day for David. On the very last day, we all gathered in the middle of the room. He pulled a chair up against me, put the spoon around my neck. Those are all scenes. All of them have a physical location at all times. In movies that's automatic. You can't point the camera and not have a place to point it at. But in storytelling, when we're speaking, when we're telling our stories, we have to keep in mind that we are the cinematographers of the story. Essentially, our eyes serve as the camera for what our audience will see. And therefore at all times, no matter what is happening within a story, our audience must be able to identify the scene, the location. Because remember, once they can identify location, they've been imbued with 1,000 adjectives. The movie continues to run in their mind. This sounds easier than it sometimes is. I was working with a guy a few months ago and he had to explain photosynthesis in the middle of his story. I know it sounds silly, but actually it was a really good metaphor. And I acknowledged, yes, photosynthesis oddly belongs in your story. He said to me, I need to explain how it works. And I said, well, I think everybody knows how photosynthesis works. And then I said, actually, I bet there's a lot of people that don't understand how photosynthesis works, sadly. I agree. We have to explain it. The problem with his story is the way he explained it was this. He was telling a beautiful story and then he said, before the story goes on, you have

## [03:04 - 06:08]

to understand how photosynthesis works. And I said, stop. You're not storytelling anymore. Now you're teaching a science lesson and nobody asked for a science lesson. So he said to me, how am I supposed to describe photosynthesis in the middle of my story? And I asked him, when did you first learn about photosynthesis? And he said, actually, I think I was in like 11th grade. I remember sitting in class and I remember the teacher with a green chalkboard describing it for me. And I thought to myself, my God, these plants for my whole life have been keeping me alive by giving me the oxygen that I need. And I've been keeping them alive by giving them the carbon dioxide that they need. And I said, there you go. You just gave me a scene. I saw you sitting in a class with a green chalkboard, yellow chalk, and I saw your mind take off at the idea that plants have been working for you all your life. That's how you describe photosynthesis with a scene, with a physical location. So throughout your story, as you start to figure out what's going to happen, the things that are going to happen will happen in places. And those places must be clearly indicated in your story. When it comes time to tell your story, that's going to be enormously valuable to you. One of the best ways to remember a story, because I don't want you to memorize, we'll talk about that later, I want you to remember your story. Remembering the scenes is a great way to remember your story. So when I'm thinking about the bad blood draw, the way I remember that story is I'm in a parking lot walking towards the building. And then I'm going to flash back to the bedroom for a minute to try to wake up Alicia to convince her to come with me. But then I'm going to be back in the parking lot. But then I'm going to flash over to the nurse who told me to grow up and be a man. And then I'm going to go back to the parking lot and into the building. And then I'm going to get in the elevator. And when I'm in the elevator, I'm going to tell the story of Sloane Kettering, which is a different scene in a different place. But then I'm going to be back in the elevator. And I'm going to leave it and walk down the hall and in the waiting room. All those people are going to be around me in the waiting room. And then I'm going to end up in the room I've been aiming at the whole time. Each one of those moments in my mind is a physical location. I remember my story by remembering the scenes that I have to pass through, the places I have to pass through. If I can pass through those places, I can get my audience to pass through those places, too. You can see them along with me. So we tell our stories not in words, not in sentences, not in paragraphs. We tell our stories in scenes, location by location by location. And that means if you tell a story that has one location, you may break that up into scenes as well. Think about the classroom scene and the spoon of power. I break it up in my mind. It is a math lesson, one scene, a reading lesson, one scene,

## [06:08 - 08:27]

a writing lesson, another scene, and then Jamie to his locker, to the library, and out the door, another scene. Four scenes in the same location. I tell a story about a time when I was seven years old that takes place entirely within the living room of my childhood home. And the way I remember those scenes is fireplace, easy chair, fireplace, couch, fireplace, cuckoo clock. Each one of those are scenes. And if I'm doing it well, my audience actually sees each one of those moments as a separate scene. They spend time in front of the fireplace. They join me on the easy chair. They go back to the fireplace with me. They're on the couch with me. They're under the cuckoo clock with me. It's one room comprised of many scenes. It allows the audience's imagination to take hold of the story and to fill it in for me. So I want you to think about stories in terms of scenes. I want you to pay attention to movies and television shows and the books that you read and pay attention to scenes. Note how we move from location to location to location. You have to do the same thing because remember, you're creating a movie in the mind of your audience. And we do that in the same way filmmakers do. We go on location. We place ourself on that location and then we tell the story of that location. One more thing. You remember at the beginning of stories, we should start as close to the end as possible? In each one of these scenes that we're going to tell, we should also start as close to the end as possible. Each one of these scenes should adhere to that same rule. Make your scenes as short as possible. Hack them with as much excitement and drama and suspense as possible by starting them as close to the end of each scene as well. When you do that, your entire story will become shorter and every moment of your story will feel more alive. Scenes. It's the way you're going to tell the stories and it's the way you're going to remember the stories.
